title.en: Classification of medical devices and in vitro diagnostic devices according to the European MDR and IVDR regulations

description.en:
An ontology designed to map the various high-risk medical devices, as well as in vitro diagnostic devices, in accordance with European regulations under the MDR and IVDR, linking devices to their manufacturers, risks, reported incidents, and corrective actions.

We do this using the LOT methodology.

Purpose and use case: to standardize and enable interoperability of information from various sources, facilitating market surveillance and, above all, regulatory compliance. With this ontology, we perform logical inferences to automatically identify risk levels or dependencies between incidents and safety actions. It can be useful for a health authority that wants to consult the safety history of a specific device, check risks, the corrective actions taken, and other information about the device.

bibliographicCitation.value: Beatriz I. S. - Classification of medical devices and in vitro diagnostic devices according to the European MDR and IVDR regulations

title.value: Classification of medical devices and in vitro diagnostic devices according to the European MDR and IVDR regulations

comment.en: Risk related to a decline in the device's quality
Risk caused by a device failure

comment.undefined: It is a mandatory communication sent by a medical device manufacturer to users (hospitals, doctors, patients) to prevent or reduce the risk of a serious incident with a device that is already on the market

comment.en: Devices that pose significant risk, often including active devices meant for monitoring or administering substances, or long-term implants, such as ventilators, infusion pumps, or bone fixation devices.
Devices with a moderate risk level, often used for short-term, non-critical, or diagnostic purposes, such as hearing aids, ultrasound equipment, or standard catheters.
It is an official communication from a manufacturer to users informing them of a safety risk with a medical device and providing instructions to prevent harm.

Devices with the highest risk profile, which are vital for supporting life, have direct contact with the heart/central circulatory system, or are permanently implanted, such as cardiac pacemakers, heart valves, and breast implants.

Any device relying on a power source other than human energy or gravity, intended to be totally or partially introduced into the body (or replacing an eye surface) via clinical intervention and intended to remain after the procedure

comment.undefined: It is any medical device that relies on a source of energy other than that generated directly by the human body or gravity.

comment.en: It is an in vitro diagnostic medical device intended by the manufacturer for use by laypersons in a home environment.

comment.fr-be: Agence Nationale de Sécurité du Médicament et des Produits de Santé

comment.en: Any reagent, instrument, software, or kit used in vitro to examine specimens from the human body, such as blood or tissue, to provide information on health status, illnesses, or congenital impairments.
An action taken to address a product problem while the device remains at the healthcare facility or user location
Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency

It is defined as any incident involving a device that directly or indirectly led, might have led, or might lead to the death of a patient/user, a serious deterioration in health (temporary/permanent), or a serious public health threat. It includes malfunctions, performance issues, and use errors.
A powered device introduced totally or partially into the body via surgery or medical intervention (including natural orifices) and intended to remain there after the procedure.
Risk that could cause harm to the patient

Any device, including partially or wholly absorbed ones, intended to be totally introduced into the human body, or to replace an epithelial/eye surface, via clinical intervention and remain there after the procedure.
It is a manufacturer’s action to fix, return, or destroy a medical device after it has left their control due to risks to safety, performance, or regulatory compliance
It is defined as a device intended to be used on one individual patient during a single procedure and then discarded. They are not intended to be reprocessed or reused.
Devices with minimal risk to patient safety, such as non-electronic walking aids, bandages, or simple surgical instruments.
It is an unplanned event that had the potential to cause, but did not result in, patient harm, injury, or damage, often due to luck or timely interception

comment.fr: Agence Fédérale des Médicaments et des Produits de Santé
